spec_match.rationale: The page now clearly probes navigator.credentials.get and labels the endpoint-backed lifecycle model, but refresh/revoke remain a showcase simulation because the agentic lifecycle details are still origin-trial/explainer-stage.

interactive.rationale: The state machine includes scenario cards, request/refresh/revoke/expire buttons, a browser API probe, account and mediation selects, TTL profiles, and an auto-refresh toggle.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The lede and controls still focus on request, issue, validate, refresh, expiry, denial, revocation, delegation, and offline-refresh behavior.

edge_cases.rationale: The demo covers short-lived tokens, provider denial, server-side revocation, delegated scopes, offline refresh failure, manual expiry, and disabled auto-refresh.

references.rationale: The concept includes a direct chromestatus.com/feature/5141084139290624 link in its see-also section.

design_system.rationale: The raw TTL hex colors were replaced with CSS-variable classes, and the added controls use explicit accessible color pairs.

openQuestions.title: Replace the refresh shim if the trial ships a browser-managed refresh contract
openQuestions.detail: The page now states that refresh is modeled with the showcase /fedcm/refresh endpoint. If Chrome 148 exposes a concrete browser-managed token refresh API, wire that API behind the existing feature-detect instead of treating refresh as a backend-only simulation.

summary: The major ChromeStatus reference gap and design-system color issue are resolved. The demo now exposes the feature-detect/fallback boundary and gives users controls for account, mediation, TTL, auto-refresh, and failure scenarios.
